1	# Proposals for issue 14268
2	
3	## Proposal 0
4	
5	# Proposal
6	
7	## Problem 
8	As the description says we don't count the actual sending characters, instead we just count the typed characters to determine a valid length.
9	
10	## Solution 1
11	
12	Count the html parsed characters and limit it to 60K as that is the limit that has been set in the backend/DB.
13	
14	---
15	
16	## Proposal 1
17	
18	## Proposal
19	
20	### Please re-state the problem that we are trying to solve in this issue.
21	
22	The composer does not prevent sending comments containing large amounts of markdown that result in a `402 Maximum Size Exceeded` error from the API endpoints.
23	
24	### What is the root cause of that problem?
25	
26	The comment length calculation does not account for the markdown conversion.
27	
28	### What changes do you think we should make in order to solve the problem?
29	
30	I propose three changes to solve this issue:
31	1. Include the markdown transformation in the `getCommentLength` function. 
32	2. Defer counting until there is a break in typing, since it's too expensive to perform for each key-press.
33	3. Abstract the calculation to a pure component, so calculating is only occurring once each time.
34	
35	A demonstration of the deferred counter can be viewed in the following video.
36	
37	The counter is normally hidden until the limit is reached, but was enabled permanently for this example:
38	
39	[Screencast from 2023-02-10 02:23:47 AM.webm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1311325/218070115-a5b19680-72af-402b-bf0e-2a0dfdcf7d3a.webm)
40	
41	By adopting these changes, we are increasing the performance of the composer beyond where it's currently at, while at the same time solving this issue.  The app is currently counting comment length four times per keypress due to re-renders of the composer components.
42	
43	See also [this screenshot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1311325/218080532-ea6371b6-54b5-4355-ad7f-1b5eccbea726.png) demonstrating the accurate character count for the OP of this issue.
44	
45	### Additional clarifications from the same user in later comments
46	
47	
48	#### Comment at https://github.com/Expensify/App/issues/14268#issuecomment-1430846150
49	
50	> It actually increases typing performance for long messages. We're currently encoding the message four times for every keypress. By  debouncing the calculation until there is a 500ms break in typing, we are eliminating any process blocking while typing characters.
51	>
52	> The plain-text message will still contain the markdown syntax. The user would have no indication why their message was not formatted as expected. In a professional context this could reflect poorly on the sender of the message.
53	>
54	> My solution also takes into account the 10,000 character plain-text conversion by abstracting the same function.  No markdown conversions would be happening in the counter beyond this amount either.
55	>
56	> I disagree. The addition of a server limit constant means it would need to be updated along with other code changes. It's a big part of the reason why we did not want to add such a constant for the other issue.  The reality is that updating the value will be overlooked and this issue will re-appear.
57	
58	
59	#### Comment at https://github.com/Expensify/App/issues/14268#issuecomment-1430873635
60	
61	> We are not actually modifying what they're sending, we're infact sending the text as it is if it's greater than 10K.
62	>
63	> It's better to let users control what they are sending rather than modifying their message without their knowledge.
64	>
65	> The conversion to plain-text at 10,000 characters is because the server has trouble processing that amount of markdown, and a workaround like that should be avoided if possible.
66	>
67	> I disagree. The addition of a server limit constant means it would need to be updated along with other code changes.
68	
69	
70	#### Comment at https://github.com/Expensify/App/issues/14268#issuecomment-1432848013
71	
72	> Thank you @francoislm
73	>
74	> I can demonstrate the performance enhancement with two videos.
75	>
76	> As you can see, the test above completely locks up my browser while performing key events due to the thousands of times the `getCommentLength` function is being invoked.  The composer has noticeable lag, even with regular typing.
77	>
78	> In the second video, we see that the optimization greatly improves performance.
79	
80	
81	#### Comment at https://github.com/Expensify/App/issues/14268#issuecomment-1435015513
82	
83	> It was already decided to [encode the comment](https://github.com/redstar504/ExpensifyApp/blob/3850a7c219328ea0ba1c9f9422ef8591ea992cae/src/libs/ReportUtils.js#L1391) to determine the comment length in the other issue.
84	>
85	> I will report back with some benchmarks so we can get a better idea of how long the markdown conversion will take with the debouncing optimization.
86	
87	
88	#### Comment at https://github.com/Expensify/App/issues/14268#issuecomment-1435294395
89	
90	> I benchmarked the string from OP at around 250ms.  It was barely noticeable while adding or deleting text as you can see in the video below.  You would need to have an unrealistic amount of markdown and time it just right to notice it.
91	>
92	> [output.webm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1311325/219794385-0413b005-6a99-473e-92cc-9b3407c2bf8c.webm)
93	
94	
95	#### Comment at https://github.com/Expensify/App/issues/14268#issuecomment-1435355054
96	
97	> Parsing such an extreme amount of Markdown from OP is indeed slower on Android. It's not really a problem because it does not block the thread. I can continue typing while the calculation happens, as you can see.
98	>
99	> [Screencast from 2023-02-17 02:30:07 PM.webm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1311325/219807978-5d87cb61-c7b7-4cbc-87d6-4fa501113e28.webm)
100	
101	
102	#### Comment at https://github.com/Expensify/App/issues/14268#issuecomment-1435388402
103	
104	> If we look at a string of `*b*` that would be allowed, there is no perceptible delay apart from the interval.
105	>
106	> [Screencast from 2023-02-17 03:17:37 PM.webm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1311325/219815360-7deb5850-d22c-4cc4-80e6-f80cabdd1b00.webm)
107	
108	---
109	
110	## Proposal 2
111	
112	# Proposal
113	
114	## Solution 2
115	
116	Send the non-parsed message if the parsed html message exceeds the 60K character limit as that is the limit that has been set in the backend/DB. Also, currently we don't parse text to MD/HTML when the text is above 10K characters so this can be a good enough solution.
117	
118	```diff
119	diff --git a/src/libs/ReportUtils.js b/src/libs/ReportUtils.js
120	index 45b9319a4..706846268 100644
121	--- a/src/libs/ReportUtils.js
122	+++ b/src/libs/ReportUtils.js
123	@@ -651,16 +651,17 @@ function buildOptimisticReportAction(sequenceNumber, text, file) {
124	     // For longer comments, skip parsing and display plaintext for performance reasons. It takes over 40s to parse a 100k long string!!
125	     const parser = new ExpensiMark();
126	     const commentText = text.length < 10000 ? parser.replace(text) : text;
127	+    const finalCommentText = commentText < 60000 ? commentText : text;
128	     const isAttachment = _.isEmpty(text) && file !== undefined;
129	     const attachmentInfo = isAttachment ? file : {};
130	-    const htmlForNewComment = isAttachment ? 'Uploading Attachment...' : commentText;
131	+    const htmlForNewComment = isAttachment ? 'Uploading Attachment...' : finalCommentText;
132	
133	     // Remove HTML from text when applying optimistic offline comment
134	     const textForNewComment = isAttachment ? '[Attachment]'
135	         : parser.htmlToText(htmlForNewComment);
136	
137	     return {
138	-        commentText,
139	+        commentText: finalCommentText,
140	         reportAction: {
141	             reportActionID: NumberUtils.rand64(),
142	             actionName: CONST.REPORT.ACTIONS.TYPE.ADDCOMMENT,
143	```
144	
145	---
146	
147	We should check the length of the HTML text before sending, and if it is greater than the message character limit then we should just send the plain text instead of the HTML text. (We do this already for text greater than 10K characters)
148	
149	@aimane-chnaif
150	
151	---
152	
153	## Proposal 3
154	
155	## Proposal
156	
157	The optimization was briefly explored in my recent [PR](https://github.com/Expensify/App/pull/14752) for counting the proper length of non-ASCII/emoji characters. I was only able to achieve it using a hook-based approach, so the change was deferred.
158	
159	Since then I have refactored it into a very simple class-based [implementation](https://github.com/redstar504/ExpensifyApp/pull/12/files).
160	
161	## Final Notes
162	
163	Consider the example of a user composing a rich message consisting of a several emojis, bilingual characters, and formatted markdown text. This comment will add up to the limit very quickly. If we don't notify the user that the message is too long before sending it, they will have no clue why it's being converted to plain text.
164	
165	The message would be full of raw markdown syntax that would likely confuse the other participant. It's better from a UX perspective to let them know it's too long and give them the opportunity to revise it before sending.
166	
167	---
168

text: Let me analyze the four proposals for issue 14268. **The Problem:** When a user types characters that include markdown syntax (like `*a*` repeated 3333 times), the character count in the UI counts the typed characters, but the backend/API has a 60,000 character limit on the *parsed HTML* version of the message. When the markdown expands (e.g., `*a*` → `a` which is longer), the message exceeds the backend limit and the API returns a "402 Maximum Size Exceeded" error. The issue is about character counting - the UI should count the HTML-converted length, not the raw text length, so the user knows when they're approaching the limit. **Proposal 0:** - Count the HTML parsed characters and limit it to 60K (the backend limit). - Very brief, no implementation details. **Proposal 1 (more detailed):** - Root cause: comment length calculation doesn't account for markdown conversion. - Three changes: 1. Include markdown transformation in `getCommentLength`. 2. Defer counting (debounce) until there's a break in typing since it's expensive. 3. Abstract to a pure component to calculate only once. - Provides performance benchmarks showing ~250ms for extreme cases. - Shows debouncing eliminates process blocking while typing. - Argues for counting against HTML length rather than silently converting to plain text. **Proposal 2:** - Different approach: if the parsed HTML exceeds 60K, send the unparsed plain text instead. - Essentially a fallback - send plain text when HTML is too long. - This doesn't actually solve the problem that the user wants to know why their message failed or was converted. It silently degrades the message. **Proposal 3:** - Focuses on refactoring the implementation to a class-based approach. - Mentions that if users aren't notified about message length, they'll be confused when their message converts to plaintext. - Less concrete about the solution. **Analysis from a maintainer's perspective:** The root cause is clear: the character count shown to the user counts raw typed characters, but the backend's limit applies to the parsed HTML output. When markdown syntax expands (e.g., `*a*` → `a`), the parsed version can exceed 60K even though the raw text is under that limit. Proposal 1 is the strongest approach because: 1. It correctly identifies the root cause and addresses it at the right layer - changing how `getCommentLength` works. 2. It acknowledges and solves the performance concern (markdown parsing is expensive) through debouncing. 3. The author provided benchmarks and video evidence to demonstrate the performance is acceptable. 4. It includes the UX consideration - users should know before sending that their message is too long, rather than having it silently converted to plaintext. 5. The "pure component" abstraction is good engineering practice. Proposal 2 is a workaround, not a fix - it silently sends unformatted text when the parsed version is too long. This degrades UX without addressing the root cause. Proposal 3 is less detailed and more about refactoring approach. Proposal 0 is too brief to evaluate seriously. So I'll select **Proposal 1** (proposal_id: 1).

{"results":[{"anchor_id":"repo://legacy","anchor_kind":"repo","content":"## Pages Router → App Router Migration Patterns (Next.js 16)

Successfully migrated a 9-route Pages Router project to App Router. Key durable patterns:

1. **`getServerSideProps`** → async server component. Use `headers()` from `next/headers` to read request headers (user-agent, cookies). Interactive features needing `useRouter` (from `next/navigation`) go in a `'use client'` wrapper component.

2. **`getStaticProps` with ISR** → server component with `fetch(..., { next: { revalidate: N } })`. Cleaner, colocated, same behavior.

3. **`getStaticPaths`** → `generateStaticParams`. Same signature (returns array of `{ param: value }`). `fallback: 'blocking'` is the default behavior in App Router (pages generate on first visit, blocking the request). `router.isFallback` check → `loading.tsx` for the loading UI.

4. **`_app.js` + `_document.js`** → single `app/layout.tsx`. `next/document` (Html, Head, Main, NextScript) is unused. Use `export const metadata: Metadata` for `<head>` content. Use `<html>` and `<body>` directly in the layout. Google Fonts preconnect links go in a `<head>` element inside the layout. Global CSS imports work the same way (import in layout).

5. **`_error.js` with `getInitialProps`** → `app/error.tsx`. Must be a `'use client'` component receiving `{ error, reset }` props. `getInitialProps` has no App Router equivalent. For status-code-based error pages, derive from `error.digest`.

6. **`404.js`** → `app/not-found.tsx`. Can be client or server component. `useRouter` from `next/navigation` for navigation.

7. **API Routes** → Route Handlers with named exports (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`). Use `NextRequest` and `NextResponse` from `next/server`. `params` is the second argument, and in Next.js 16 it's a `Promise<{ id: string }>` — must be awaited. Unsupported methods return 405 automatically by Next.js (no need to manually return 405).

8. **Type migration**: All `.js` files → `.tsx`/`.ts` with proper interfaces for props, posts, comments, and API response debugging a rendering flicker (content flashes one style then snaps to another), the root cause is often a markup mismatch between client-side parse and server-side re-parse of the same input. The client renders its version first, then replaces it with the server's version once the round-trip completes — producing a visible flash.

Diagnostic pattern: reproduce in offline mode. If the flicker disappears (the "wrong" style persists indefinitely), it confirms the server returns structurally different markup that the client's renderer processes differently. The fix is to make the client's parser produce markup consistent with the server's output, not to mask the flicker with CSS.

This applies broadly to any rich-text/render system where the same source text is parsed independently on two sides of a network boundary — not just chat markdown.","domain":"project","drawer_id":"drawer_project_software_engineering_b497f911","emotions":["determ"],"entities":["WHE","THE","DIA","DJC","CSS","THI"],"field":"general","flags":["DECISION","TECHNICAL"],"importance_stars":4,"memory_kind":"evidence","room":"software-engineering","route":{"confidence":0,"reason":"global search: no taxonomy keyword match","room":null,"wing":null},"similarity":-0.09841597080230713,"source_file":"engineering-decision","topics":["when","debugging","a","rendering","flicker","content","flashes","one","style","then","snaps","to","another","root","cause","is","often","markup","mismatch","between","client","side","parse","server","re","of","same","input","renders","its","version","first","replaces","it","s","once","round","trip","completes","producing","visible","flash","diagnostic","pattern","reproduce","in","offline","mode","if","disappears","wrong","persists","indefinitely","confirms","returns","structurally","different","renderer","processes","differently","fix","make","parser","produce","consistent","output","not","mask","css","applies","broadly","any","rich","text","render","system","where","source","parsed","independently","on","two","sides","network","boundary","just","chat","markdown"],"wing":"project"},{"anchor_id":"repo://legacy","anchor_kind":"repo","content":"In Next.js 16 canary, `updateTag(tag)` provides read-your-own-writes semantics for a Server Action (current user sees fresh data immediately) but does NOT purge the shared server cache — other users still get stale data until their cache expires naturally. To guarantee NO stale content is served to any user on the next page load, use `revalidateTag(tag, { expire: 0 })` instead — this immediately expires the shared cache entry, so the very next server render blocks on fetching fresh data rather than serving a stale copy. The key distinction: updateTag = current user only, revalidateTag with expire:0 = global purge. Combine with redirect() after the Server Action to force a full server render that hits the now-expired cache.

**Why:** The common Next.js 16 guidance says "use updateTag in Server Actions" but that recommendation is for the read-your-own-writes case (e.g., a dashboard where the mutating user should see their change immediately after a client-side refresh). It is NOT sufficient when the requirement is that no user ever sees stale content — e.g., after creating a blog post, the public listing page must show the new post immediately to ALL visitors.

**How to apply:** When building mutation Server Actions that must guarantee fresh data for every subsequent page visitor:
1. Call `revalidateTag('your-tag', { expire: 0 })` — the `{ expire: 0 }` tells Next.js to expire the cache entry immediately
2. Then `redirect('/target-page')` to force a full server-side render of the target page
3. Use `updateTag('your-tag')` only when you need read-your-own-writes within the same action (less common — most Server Actions redirect, which already creates a new request)","domain":"project","drawer_id":"drawer_reference_nextjs_654fc4f4","emotions":["determ"],"entities":["FKC","NEX","SER","ACT","NOT","BUC","BOC","THE","COM","WHY","LKC","ALL","HOW","WHE","CAL","USE"],"field":"general","flags":["DECISION","ORIGIN","TECHNICAL"],"importance_stars":4,"memory_kind":"evidence","room":"nextjs","route":{"confidence":0,"reason":"global search: no taxonomy keyword match","room":null,"wing":null},"similarity":-0.10543251037597656,"source_file":"revalidateTag expire:0 pattern discovered during post-creation Server Action implementation on Next.js 16.2.0-canary.41","topics":["in","next","js","16","canary","updatetag","tag","provides","read","your","own","writes","semantics","a","server","action","current","user","sees","fresh","data","immediately","but","does","not","purge","shared","cache","other","users","still","get","stale","until","their","expires","naturally","to","guarantee","no","content","is","served","any","on","page","load","revalidatetag","expire","0","instead","entry","so","very","render","blocks","fetching","rather","than","serving","copy","key","distinction","only","global","combine","redirect","after","force","full","hits","now","expired","common","guidance","says","actions","recommendation","case","e","g","dashboard","where","mutating","should","see","change","client","side","refresh","it","sufficient","when","requirement","ever","creating","blog","post","public","listing","must","show","new","all","visitors","how","apply","building","mutation","every","subsequent","visitor","1","call","tells","2","then","target","of","3","you","need","within","same","less","most","which","already","creates","request"],"wing":"reference"},{"anchor_id":"repo://legacy","anchor_kind":"repo","content":"When judging feature proposals as a maintainer, evaluate on five axes:

1. **Root cause clarity** — Does the proposal correctly identify what's broken or missing, not just label it "new feature" or dismiss it? A good root cause frames the UX gap, even for feature requests.

2. **Solution focus** — Does the solution solve only the stated problem without scope creep? Extra refactors, secondary entry points, or "further enhancements" signal the author lost sight of the issue boundary. A maintainer accepts minimal diffs.

3. **Pattern correctness** — Does the proposal reference existing codebase APIs, components, and conventions correctly (e.g., correct prop names, correct existing utility functions, correct component behavior like ConfirmModal already having isVisible)? Inferred or made-up API names are a red flag.

4. **Completeness** — Can a developer implement from the proposal alone? Vague skeletons or missing steps (navigation behavior, translation keys, confirmation flow) mean the proposal is half-baked.

5. **Scope control** — Proposals that add the minimum code win over proposals that touch more files or add abstractions "for future use." A common modal refactor belongs in a follow-up, not the first fix.

Use these axes to rank proposals rather than picking by verbosity or apparent thoroughness — a concise, correct proposal beats a verbose one with pattern proposals","topics":["when","judging","feature","proposals","as","a","maintainer","evaluate","on","five","axes","1","root","cause","clarity","does","proposal","correctly","identify","what","s","broken","or","missing","not","just","label","it","new","dismiss","good","frames","ux","gap","even","requests","2","solution","focus","solve","only","stated","problem","without","scope","creep","extra","refactors","secondary","entry","points","further","enhancements","signal","author","lost","sight","of","issue","boundary","accepts","minimal","diffs","3","pattern","correctness","reference","existing","codebase","apis","components","conventions","e","g","correct","prop","names","utility","functions","component","behavior","like","confirmmodal","already","having","isvisible","inferred","made","up","api","are","red","flag","4","completeness","can","developer","implement","from","alone","vague","skeletons","steps","navigation","translation","keys","confirmation","flow","mean","is","half","baked","5","control","add","minimum","code","win","touch","more","files","abstractions","future","common","modal","refactor","belongs","in","follow","first","fix","these","to","rank","rather","than","picking","by","verbosity","apparent","thoroughness","concise","beats","verbose","one","mistakes"],"wing":"project"},{"anchor_id":"repo://legacy","anchor_kind":"repo","content":"In Next.js 16 canary, schedule post-response background work in Server Actions via `after(fn)` from `next/server` — replaces the older `waitUntil()` which is no longer exported. `after()` accepts an async callback that runs after the response is sent, so the client gets an immediate response while the background work continues.

For non-urgent background sync with eventual consistency (e.g., catalog refresh after ERP sync), pair `after()` with `revalidateTag(tag, 'default')` — the `'default'` profile tells Next.js to re-apply the standard stale/revalidate lifecycle to the revalidated content. This is distinct from:
- `updateTag(tag)` — read-your-own-writes within the same action, doesn't purge the shared server cache
- `revalidateTag(tag, { expire: 0 })` — immediate global cache purge (for when no user should see stale data)

Use the `'default'` profile when stale data is acceptable for a brief window and the priority is a responsive admin experience.

**Why:** The `after()` API is the canonical way to do fire-and-forget background work in Next.js 16. Using `revalidateTag(tag, 'default')` instead of `updateTag` or `{ expire: 0 }` gives the right trade-off for read-heavy catalog sync: the admin gets immediate form response, the background sync runs without blocking any request, and cache invalidation is graceful with standard lifecycle management.","domain":"project","drawer_id":"drawer_reference_nextjs_e688abac","emotions":["determ"],"entities":["FKC","NEX","SER","ACT","FOR","ERP","THI","USE","WHY","THE","API","USI"],"field":"general","flags":["DECISION","TECHNICAL"],"importance_stars":4,"memory_kind":"evidence","room":"nextjs","route":{"confidence":0,"reason":"global search: no taxonomy keyword match","room":null,"wing":null},"similarity":-0.14173805713653564,"source_file":"Admin product catalog page — after() + revalidateTag pattern for background sync","topics":["in","next","js","16","canary","schedule","post","response","background","work","server","actions","via","after","fn","from","replaces","older","waituntil","which","is","no","longer","exported","accepts","an","async","callback","runs","sent","so","client","gets","immediate","while","continues","non","urgent","sync","eventual","consistency","e","g","catalog","refresh","erp","pair","revalidatetag","tag","default","profile","tells","to","re","apply","standard","stale","revalidate","lifecycle","revalidated","content","distinct","updatetag","read","your","own","writes","within","same","action","doesn","t","purge","shared","cache","expire","0","global","when","user","should","see","data","acceptable","a","brief","window","priority","responsive","admin","experience","api","canonical","way","do","fire","forget","using","instead","of","or","gives","right","trade","off","heavy","form","without","blocking","any","request","invalidation","graceful","management"],"wing":"reference"}]}

text:
The mempal results aren't directly relevant to this issue 14268, but the "When judging feature proposals as a maintainer, evaluate on five axes" entry is useful framing.

Now let me analyze the four proposals thoroughly:

**Proposal 0:**
- Very brief - just "count the html parsed characters and limit it to 60K"
- No root cause analysis
- No implementation details
- No performance considerations
- Grade: Too vague to implement from

**Proposal 1:**
- Root cause: "The comment length calculation does not account for the markdown conversion" — correctly identifies the core issue
- Three concrete changes:
  1. Include markdown transformation in `getCommentLength`
  2. Debounce counting to avoid performance issues from expensive markdown parsing
  3. Abstract to a pure component for efficiency
- Provides benchmarks: ~250ms for extreme cases
- Shows performance videos demonstrating it works
- Argues for user-facing character counting rather than silent conversion
- Addresses the 10K character plain-text conversion consideration
- Provides follow-up clarification with evidence
- The author engaged thoroughly with reviewers and addressed concerns

**Proposal 2:**
- Different approach: silently send unparsed text when HTML exceeds limit
- This is a *workaround*, not a fix — the user still doesn't know why their formatting was lost
- Doesn't address counting in the UI
- The one-line code change is minimal but doesn't solve the UX problem
- Sends plaintext with raw markdown syntax visible to recipients — bad UX
- Makes no attempt to inform the user about the length limit

**Proposal 3:**
- Focuses on refactoring to class-based implementation
- Less concrete about the actual solution
- Notes the UX concern but doesn't provide a complete solution to the character counting issue
- More about implementation approach than solving the stated problem

**Maintainer's evaluation:**

Evaluating against the five axes from the mempal entry:

1. **Root cause clarity:** 
   - P0: Minimal — "we don't count the actual sending characters"
   - P1: Excellent — "The comment length calculation does not account for the markdown conversion." Clearly identifies the mismatch.
   - P2: Implicit — the root cause is the limit being exceeded, but frames it differently (send plaintext instead)
   - P3: Not really stated

2. **Solution focus:**
   - P0: Too vague to evaluate
   - P1: Focused — changes `getCommentLength` to count HTML, adds debouncing for performance, abstracts to pure component. All changes directly serve the stated goal.
   - P2: Misaligned — solves the API error but doesn't solve the user-facing problem (the user still can't tell their message is too long)
   - P3: Unclear scope

3. **Pattern correctness:**
   - P1: References `getCommentLength` (existing function), proposes debouncing (standard React pattern), pure components (standard pattern). References the existing 10K plaintext conversion. Author clearly knows the codebase.
   - P2: References `buildOptimisticReportAction` and `parser.replace(text)` — knows the codebase, but the approach is a band-aid.

4. **Completeness:**
   - P0: Not implementable from the description
   - P1: Detailed enough to implement — three concrete changes, referenced existing functions, provided benchmark data, addressed edge cases (10K limit interaction)
   - P2: Complete for what it proposes (one-line diff), but the proposal itself is incomplete as a solution to the issue
   - P3: Vague

5. **Scope control:**
   - P1: Three well-scoped changes that directly solve the problem. The debouncing and pure component abstraction are justified by the performance cost of markdown parsing.
   - P2: Minimal diff, but solves the wrong aspect of the problem
   - P0/P3: Not applicable due to vagueness

**Final decision:** Proposal 1 is clearly the strongest. It correctly diagnoses the root cause (counting raw text vs. HTML), provides a complete solution with performance considerations, and addresses the UX properly. The author demonstrated thorough engagement with the issue, provided benchmarks, and addressed reviewer concerns about performance.

Proposal 2 is a band-aid that would result in users sending messages with raw markdown syntax visible to recipients — degrading the experience for both sender and receiver. It doesn't solve the core issue.

Proposal 3 is too vague.

Proposal 0 is too brief.

**Selected: Proposal 1 (proposal_id: 1)**

Now I have all the context I need. Let me analyze the four proposals systematically and make my decision. ## Analysis **The problem:** When composing messages with markdown (e.g., `*a*` × 3333), the UI counts raw typed characters, but the backend enforces a 60,000-character limit on the *parsed HTML* output (`a` × 3333). This causes a silent "402 Maximum Size Exceeded" API failure with no user feedback. **Proposal 0** — Too vague to evaluate. Just "count HTML parsed characters." No root cause, no implementation detail, no performance consideration. **Proposal 1** — Correct root cause ("comment length calculation does not account for markdown conversion"), with three concrete, scoped changes: (a) include markdown transformation in `getCommentLength`, (b) debounce the expensive calculation, (c) abstract to a pure component. Author provided benchmarks (~250ms for extreme cases), performance videos, and directly addressed reviewer concerns about mobile performance. Properly considers the existing 10K plaintext-conversion code path. **Proposal 2** — Sends unparsed plaintext when HTML exceeds 60K. This is a workaround, not a fix: it silently strips all formatting without informing the user. Recipients see raw `*markdown*` syntax — degrading the experience for both parties. Doesn't address character counting at all. **Proposal 3** — Vague scope. Focuses on class-based refactoring without clearly solving the stated problem. Not implementable from the description alone. **Winner: Proposal 1** — It is the only proposal that correctly identifies the root cause, provides a complete and implementable solution, addresses the performance cost of markdown parsing head-on, gives users visibility into the limit, and stays scoped to the actual problem. Let me save the decision.

output: File created successfully at: /home/user/workspace/manager_decisions.json (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

text: The file has been written successfully. Let me provide the user with a summary of my decision.

Decision saved to `manager_decisions.json`.

**Selected: Proposal 1** for issue 14268.

### Rationale

| Axis | Assessment |
|---|---|
| **Root cause clarity** | Correctly identifies the mismatch: `getCommentLength` counts raw characters, but the backend limit applies to parsed HTML. |
| **Solution focus** | Three tightly scoped changes — all directly needed: transform in `getCommentLength`, debounce for performance, pure component to avoid redundant re-calculations. |
| **Pattern correctness** | References real existing functions (`getCommentLength`, the 10K plaintext shortcut) and proposes standard React patterns (debouncing, pure components). The author clearly knows the codebase. |
| **Completeness** | Implementable from the description alone, with benchmark data (~250ms worst case), video evidence, and edge-case handling (10K plaintext interaction). |
| **Scope control** | Adds the minimum changes needed. The debouncing is justified by the measurable cost of markdown parsing — it's not scope creep. |

**Why not Proposal 2:** It silently sends unformatted plaintext when the limit is exceeded. Recipients see raw `*markdown syntax*` in their chat — the issue explicitly says users should be told the message is too long *before* sending, not have their formatting stripped without notice.

Before stopping, save only durable engineering decisions or reusable debugging lessons to mempal. Include rationale and search first to avoid duplicates. Never store benchmark answers, accepted proposal numbers, hidden-test guesses, raw transcripts, or task-specific output that would reveal an answer on rerun. If nothing reusable was decided, just stop.

The specific reasoning about markdown vs HTML character counting being a server/client parse mismatch — that's related to the render flicker debugging pattern already stored in `drawer_project_software_engineering_b497f911`.

Nothing truly new or reusable here that isn't already captured. I'll just stop.

Nothing new to save here. The five-axes evaluation framework I used is already stored in mempal (`drawer_project_software_engineering_c507ae4d`), and the client/server parse-mismatch pattern is covered by `drawer_project_software_engineering_b497f911`.
